items.q: What is the key structural difference between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells?
items.correct: Eukaryotes have a membrane-bound nucleus and membrane-bound organelles; prokaryotes do not.
items.misconception: Prokaryotes have no DNA — that's why they have no nucleus.
items.random_wrong: Prokaryotes are always smaller than 1 micrometre and have no cytoplasm.
items.why: Students see 'no nucleus' and think 'no DNA', missing that prokaryotes still have DNA — it's just free in the cytoplasm.

items.q: How is sucrose loaded into the phloem at a source?
items.correct: By active transport (using ATP) via companion cells, against a concentration gradient.
items.misconception: Sucrose diffuses passively into the phloem because it is more concentrated outside.
items.random_wrong: Sucrose is pushed into the phloem by turgor pressure from leaf cells.
items.why: Students don't realise translocation requires energy at the loading step; they think 'flow' implies passive movement throughout.

items.correct: Each new DNA molecule contains one original (parental) strand and one newly synthesised strand.
items.misconception: One daughter molecule contains both original strands; the other contains both new strands.
items.random_wrong: DNA replication produces two identical fully new molecules with no original material.
items.why: Students confuse semi-conservative with conservative replication — they don't grasp that each daughter molecule is a hybrid of old and new strands, as Meselson-Stahl demonstrated.
Correct answer: Glycosidic. “Peptide” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's Addition of water. “Loss of water” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
